data.full_text: A man lies on his back in a shallow pit, knees drawn up, body dusted in red ochre. Above him, a low mound of earth, what archaeologists call a kuran, rises from the grasslands near the city of Samara. A copper scepter rests beside his hand. No inscription, no name, no written language to preserve whatever prayers or curses followed him into the ground. He was buried sometime around 3000 B.CE CE in the classic Yamna position, facing east toward the rising sun over the vulga. For a century, burials like this one defined what we thought we knew about one of the most consequential populations in human history. Nomadic herders, riders, and wagon builders. The people who around 5,000 years ago supposedly poured out of the pontic Caspian step and rewrote the genetic story of an entire continent. For nearly a decade, ancient DNA seemed to confirm every piece of that narrative. Clean data, clear ancestry, case closed. Except the case was built on a model that turns out to have been structurally incomplete since the day it was published. And the evidence that broke it open was not some fringe finding. It was the largest ancient DNA study of the step ever conducted, led by the same lab that built the original model in the first place. The story that held from 2015 onward, went like this. Two landmark studies dropped almost simultaneously. One led by Wolf Gang Hawk, the other by Morton Allen, both drawing on the revolution in ancient DNA sequencing that had transformed human genetics in under 5 years. Their conclusion, supported by dozens of Bronze Age genomes extracted from bones and teeth across Europe and Central Asia, was dramatic. The Yamnia carried a genetic signature that appeared to be a roughly equal blend of two deep ancestral populations. About half their DNA came from Eastern hunter gatherers or EHG, people who had roamed the forests and river valleys of Eastern Europe since the end of the ice age. The other half came from Cauasus hunter gatherers or CHG, populations rooted in the mountain valleys of Georgia and the southern Cauasus thousands of kilome to the south. A clean almost symmetrical mix. two ancient lineages merging on the step to produce something new. And the implications of that new thing were staggering. Between roughly 3000 and 2,300 BCE, the Yamna and their descendants appeared to have replaced vast portions of the indigenous population across the entire European continent. In Britain, in Iberia, in Scandinavia, step ancestry surged among corded wear populations in central Europe. Up to 75% of ancestry derived from Yamnia related sources. The genetic term for their signature western step herder ancestry became a fixture in every paper on European prehistory and the Yamna became synonymous with the origin of the Indo-Uropean language family. The family spoken today by roughly 46% of the world's population from Icelandic to Hindi, Portuguese to Poshto. David Reich at Harvard called the Yamna a single genetically coherent group. David Anthony at Hartwick College, who had spent decades studying the step, argued they were the most likely source of protoindo-uropean, the reconstructed ancestor of languages now spoken on every inhabited continent. The genome of the man in that kiran near Samara seemed to contain the answer to one of the oldest questions in historical linguistics. A question scholars had been debating since William Jones first noticed the resemblance between Sanskrit and Greek in 1786. That sense of resolution was powerful. It was also premature because the model had a quiet structural problem and it sat in plain sight in the Y chromosome data that everyone could see but few wanted to confront directly. The Yamnia males who had been sampled overwhelmingly carried a specific YDNA HLO group R1BZ2103. This is a paternal lineage, a genetic surname passed from father to son, unchanged across dozens of generations. If the Yamna were truly the direct paternal ancestors of the cultures that spread step ancestry across Europe, you would expect to find this lineage in their supposed descendants. You would expect it in the corded wear culture, the first major archaeological complex in central and northern Europe to show heavy step ancestry. You would expect it in modern Western Europeans, the populations that carry the most Yamnia derived DNA on the planet. It is not there. Corded wear males carry R1A M417 and R1B L-51. These are sister lineages to the Yamnia Haplo group, not daughter lineages. They share a deep common ancestor, branching apart sometime before 4,000 B.CE. But one does not descend from the other. Barry Kunliff flagged this disconnect. So did Leo Clain, the veteran Russian archaeologist who had been skeptical of the step migration model from the beginning. And in 2023, Vulkar Hay and colleagues at the University of Helsinki stated it with no ambiguity. The specific paternal haplo group most common in sampled Yamna males cannot be found in modern Western Europeans or in corded wear males. What this means in plain terms is that the men buried in Yamna kirens on the vulga were not the direct patrineal forefathers of the people who transformed Europe's gene pool. Their autotosomal DNA the full genome signature matches. Their paternal line does not. and paternal lines are the one thing that should be most resistant to disruption in a patrineal exogamous society which is exactly what the Yamnia appear to have been. The field worked around this. David Anthony, one of the foremost scholars of Yamnia culture, proposed that dominant R1B clans might have been displaced by rising R1A lineages during internal social upheaval, a kind of paternal revolution within a few generations. Others suggested the problem was simpler. The sampled Yamna came overwhelmingly from the eastern part of the range around Samara and the lower Vulga. The western Yamnia, the ones closer to Europe, might have carried entirely different paternal lineages. These are reasonable hypotheses. Neither has been confirmed. And while the debate simmered in specialist journals, the public-f facing narrative continued to present the Yam Naya as the uncomplicated origin point of a continental transformation. Building a migration narrative from one corner of a 5,000 km cultural horizon is like reconstructing the history of the Roman Empire from a single garrison in Britain. You might get the garrison right, you will get Roman wrong. Then came the study that redrrew everything. In 2024, ISF Lazeritis at Harvard working with Nick Patterson, David Anthony, Leonid Vazoff, David Reich, and a vast international team spanning universities from Copenhagen to Kev to Samara assembled ancient DNA from 435 individuals. 299 of those genomes were reported for the first time. The samples spanned from roughly 6,400 to 2000 B.CEE covering the entire enolithic and early Bronze Age of the Pontic Caspian region and beyond. The paper went through full peer review and was published in Nature in February 2025. This was not a blog post. This was not a preprint left to gather dust. This was the formal corrected definitive report. And what it revealed was not a minor adjustment. It was a demolition of the two component model that had anchored the field for a decade. The Yamna did not form from that neat two ancestry blend. Instead, the team identified three previously unknown genetic gradients stretching across the step and caucases, population corridors they called clines. The first and most consequential they named the Cauasus lower vulga or CLV. This was a genetic gradient running from Neolithic Armenia in the south. Communities at sites like Aknashen and Masses Blur to a site called Berajnovka on the lower vulga in the north. People along this corridor carried heavy Caucasus huntergatherer ancestry but also Anatolian Neolithic ancestry, a component the old model had completely missed. Gene flow along the CLV was birectional north and south creating intermediate populations like the MOP people of the north Caucasus and the communities at Ramonty on the open step. The second Klein, the Vulga Klein, formed as CLV people mixed with populations further up river who had more traditional EHG ancestry, producing genetically hypervariable populations at sites like Kavalinsk. These were the very populations earlier studies had placed at the center of Yamna origins. Under the new model, they turn out to be a parallel branch, not the trunk. The third Klein is the one that changes the story. CLV people carrying both Cauasus Neolithic and lower vulga ancestry moved west. They crossed into what is now Ukraine and mixed with local hunter gatherers along the Nepro and Dawn rivers. People whose genetic signature had persisted in that region for millennia, stretching back to a site called Golubaya on the Middle Dawn, dated to roughly 5,600 B.CE. de these were not farmers. They were the last descendants of Ice Age foraging populations who had never adopted agriculture, never built permanent settlements, and never encountered the genetic influence of the Neolithic Revolution that had transformed Western Europe thousands of years earlier. This fusion, CLV migrants meeting deeprooted local foragers produced the Seredni ste culture named after an island on the Nepro where its remains were first identified. And it is from Sednney ste communities arrayed along this Nippro cline that the direct ancestors of the Yamna formed around 4,000 B.CE. If you are finding this channel through this video, the thread connecting these CLV people to the Hittites of Bronze Age Anatolia through a completely separate migration route is something we are building a dedicated video around. That connection might be the most significant finding in this entire study. Subscribe so you do not miss it. The numbers here deserve their own moment. CLV people contributed roughly 4fifths of Yamna ancestry. Not half from the forests, half from the mountains, 4-fifths from a single complex multissourced population corridor stretching over a thousand km from Armenia to the vulga. The remaining fifth came from Ukrainian Neolithic hunter gatherers absorbed during the westward push. The old 50/50 model was not slightly miscalibrated. It was fundamentally wrong about how the Yam Naya came into being. And what happened next makes the revision even more striking. The team used a computational method called Hapne LD designed specifically to infer effective population size changes from low coverage ancient DNA. They split their core Yamna samples. 104 genetically near identical individuals sampled from the Carpathian basin all the way to the Alai Mountains into two groups based on dating the earlier 300 years and the later 300 years. Then they overlaid the two demographic trajectories. What appeared was an extraordinary population explosion between roughly 36 to 42 B.CE and 33 to 74 B.CE. The effective population size went from a few thousand to an order of magnitude larger. A community that could have occupied a handful of neighboring villages along the lower Denipro became a demographic force numbering in the tens of thousands within a few centuries. And as they expanded, they maintained startling genetic homogeneity. Core Yamna buried in Hungary look essentially identical to core Yamna buried near the borders of Mongolia and China. That does not happen in populations absorbing locals as they go. That is a population expanding faster than it can mix. This uniformity contrasts sharply with the Serednist who were scattered along the neproine genetically variable not yet consolidated into a single tight cluster. The Yamnia in genetic terms are the product of a bottleneck followed by an eruption. A small cohesive subset of Serednist descendants that locked into a specific genetic profile and then swept outward across half of Eurasia. Three consequences follow each rewriting a different chapter of the old narrative. First, the evidence shows that Yamna formation was geographically specific. It did not happen across the entire step in some broad gradual blending process. It happened in the neprodon region among serednist communities who were themselves a product of the CLV expansion. Mikaila on the lowered nepro in modern keren oblast has been identified as one of the core yamnia sites dated to roughly 3,600 to 3,400 B.CEE. The vulgar populations that earlier models had placed at the origin sites like Kavalinsk turn out to be a side channel genetically distinct not ancestral. Second, this rules out the idea that the Yamna were the original protoindo-uropean speakers in any straightforward sense. CLV ancestry appears not only in the Yamna on the step but also in Bronze Age central Anatolians at least onetenth of the ancestry of populations who spoke Hittite. Hittite is the earliest attested Indo-Uropean language belonging to the Anatolian branch which split off from the rest of the family before any other documented divergence. If both the Yamna and the Hittite speakers received CLV ancestry through completely different geographic routes, one west across the step, the other south through Mesopotamia, then the linguistic unity predates the Yamna entirely. It sits with the CLV people sometime between 4,400 and 4,000 BCE. The Yamna were not the beginning of the Indo-Uropean story. They were chapter 2. Third, this implies a level of social and demographic organization among CLV communities that archaeology has barely begun to examine. A population corridor sustaining two-way genetic exchange across mountain passes and open grassland for centuries, generating the demographic raw material for multiple downstream expansions in different directions. This is not a picture of isolated nomadic bands stumbling into favorable conditions. This is a network. But the picture remains deeply incomplete. The paternal hapla group problem laid out earlier has not been resolved by this study. Lazeritus confirmed the genetic tightness of the core Yamnia, but that only deepens the question. Pac and colleagues found that the earliest corded wear males in Bohemia carried R1BL151, a lineage that shares a common ancestor with the Yamna R1 BZ2103, but branched off before either culture existed, suggesting the split happened among preyamnia step groups that remain poorly sampled. Morton Allen Tooff's 2024 work suggested the corded wear formed rapidly through genetic amalgamation of steprelated groups with the globular empora culture of Eastern Europe, not through direct Yamnia migration. This matters because it means the largest ancestry shift in European prehistory may not have been driven by the Yamnia at all, but by a related population that coalesed independently. Clay raises another point that no one has adequately answered. If the Yamna migration was the primary driver, you would expect the highest concentration of step derived DNA closest to the Yamna homeland, decreasing with distance. Instead, step ancestry peaks in Scandinavia and the Baltic and is comparatively lower in Hungary, right at the western edge of the Yamna range. The gradient runs the wrong direction for a simple westward wave. something more complex happened. Something involving multiple source populations, differential mixing rates, founder effects at the leading edge, and possibly the selective survival of certain communities over others as they moved into forested environments radically different from the dryep. The Lazerites team locates the protoindo Ananatolian homeland in the CLV region and the protoindo-uropean homeland specifically in the Seredni Stak culture. But they acknowledge that which CLV subgroup gave rise to the Yamna ancestors remains uncertain. The Klein is long. Sampling is still sparse in critical stretches and the serid stems themselves were genetically diverse enough that pinpointing the exact founding cluster is not yet possible. Lazeritas put it carefully in an interview with Harets. There was nothing inherently superior about the CLV people. They had the right technology at the right time and a small random group that emerged just after 4,000 B.CE CE had an outsiz impact on human history through a combination of luck and a few favorable cultural features. There is also the question of what triggered the explosion itself. The Yamna adopted wheeled transport and intensive mobile pastoralism at roughly the same time their population surged. Oxdrawn wagons made it possible for the first time to carry water, supplies, and shelter into deep step that had previously been inaccessible for yearround habitation. Cattle could be herded across vast distances. Seasonal camps could become seasonal territories. Whether this technological package enabled the demographic surge directly or whether both innovations and population growth were symptoms of a deeper organizational shift in how Serednishi communities managed herds, territory and kinship alliances remains an open question. A study published in Nature in January 2024 found that Yamna associated gene variants significantly increasing the risk of multiple sclerosis were introduced to northwestern Europe during the Bronze Age affecting over 2 and a half million people today. Whatever advantage those immune system variants conferred on the open step strong enough to be selected for despite the autoimmune cost, we are still living with the consequences. What we do know is that a population small enough to fit inside a single river valley living along the lowered nepro sometime around 3750 B.CE underwent a demographic event so extreme that their genetic signature is now detectable in people living from Reikavic to Dhaka from the highlands of Scotland to the river plains of northern India. Right now, in labs between Cambridge, Massachusetts, and Copenhagen, Denmark, ancient DNA teams are still processing bones pulled from the CLV region. Every new genome narrows the sharpens the timeline, and pushes the origin of the languages spoken by nearly half of all living humans further back and further south than anyone imagined a decade ago. Somewhere in a university freezer, there is a petrus bone from a seredney stew woman or a CLV herder along the lower vulga that has not been sequenced yet. When it is, it might answer the question that this entire field is now circling. The Yamnia are not gone from this story, but they are no longer where it starts.

enough that pinpointing the exact
founding cluster is not yet possible.
Lazeritas put it carefully in an
interview with Harets. There was nothing
inherently superior about the CLV
people. They had the right technology at
the right time and a small random group
that emerged just after 4,000 B.CE CE
had an outsiz impact on human history
through a combination of luck and a few
favorable cultural features. There is
also the question of what triggered the
explosion itself. The Yamna adopted
wheeled transport and intensive mobile
pastoralism at roughly the same time
their population surged. Oxdrawn wagons
made it possible for the first time to
carry water, supplies, and shelter into
deep step that had previously been
inaccessible for yearround habitation.
Cattle could be herded across vast
distances.
Seasonal camps could become seasonal
territories. Whether this technological
package enabled the demographic surge
directly or whether both innovations and
population growth were symptoms of a
deeper organizational shift in how
Serednishi communities managed herds,
territory and kinship alliances remains
an open question. A study published in
Nature in January 2024 found that Yamna
associated gene variants significantly
increasing the risk of multiple
sclerosis were introduced to
northwestern Europe during the Bronze
Age affecting over 2 and a half million
people today. Whatever advantage those
immune system variants conferred on the
open step strong enough to be selected
for despite the autoimmune cost, we are
still living with the consequences.
What we do know is that a population
small enough to fit inside a single
river valley living along the lowered
nepro sometime around 3750 B.CE
underwent a demographic event so extreme
that their genetic signature is now
detectable in people living from
Reikavic to Dhaka from the highlands of
Scotland to the river plains of northern
India. Right now, in labs between
Cambridge, Massachusetts, and
Copenhagen, Denmark, ancient DNA teams
are still processing bones pulled from
the CLV region. Every new genome narrows
the sharpens the timeline, and pushes
the origin of the languages spoken by
nearly half of all living humans further
back and further south than anyone
imagined a decade ago.
Somewhere in a university freezer, there
is a petrus bone from a seredney stew
woman or a CLV herder along the lower
vulga that has not been sequenced yet.
When it is, it might answer the question
that this entire field is now circling.
The Yamnia are not gone from this story,
but they are no longer where it starts.
